answers.intake_q1: When did the elbow pain start?
answers.intake_q2: Can you describe where exactly on your elbow the pain is located?
answers.intake_q3: What were you doing when the pain started, and have you had any recent injuries or repetitive activities involving your elbow?
answers.intake_a3: I was playing tennis when the pain started
answers.intake_q4: Any swelling, redness, or warmth around the elbow?
answers.intake_q5: Any numbness, tingling, or weakness in your arm or hand?
answers.intake_q6: Any difficulty moving your elbow or pain when you try to bend or straighten it?
answers.intake_q7: Any pain when you grip or lift objects, especially during activities like holding a tennis racket?
answers.intake_q8: Have you tried any treatments or medications for your elbow pain so far?
answers.intake_q9: Any fever, chills, or other systemic symptoms since the pain started?
answers.intake_q10: Any previous history of elbow problems or other joint issues?

note.soap:
S: 
The patient is a 45-year-old female presenting with a 3-week history of pain localized to the outside of her elbow. The pain began while playing tennis and is associated with pain when lifting objects, particularly during activities such as holding a tennis racket. She denies any swelling, redness, warmth, numbness, tingling, weakness, or difficulty moving the elbow. She has not experienced any systemic symptoms like fever or chills. Initial treatment included icing on the first day only. There is no prior history of elbow or joint problems. Patient is not pregnant.

O: 
Phone evaluation only; no physical examination or vital signs available.

A: 
The clinical presentation is consistent with lateral epicondylitis (tennis elbow), characterized by lateral elbow pain exacerbated by gripping and lifting activities, with onset related to repetitive wrist extension during tennis. 
Differential: 
1. Lateral epicondylitis (tennis elbow) 
2. Radial tunnel syndrome 
3. Osteoarthritis of the elbow 
4. Referred pain from cervical radiculopathy 

P: 
 - First-line medication: Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) such as ibuprofen or naproxen for pain relief and inflammation reduction. 
 - Alternatives: Acetaminophen for pain control if NSAIDs are contraindicated or not tolerated. 
 - Medication safety considerations: Use NSAIDs with caution in patients with gastrointestinal, renal, or cardiovascular risk factors; ensure dosing does not exceed recommended limits. Acetaminophen should be used within safe daily limits to avoid hepatotoxicity. 
 - Return precautions: Advise the patient to seek care if pain worsens, if there is new onset of numbness, tingling, weakness, swelling, redness, or warmth around the elbow, or if systemic symptoms such as fever develop. 
 - Testing: Not indicated at this time based on history; consider imaging or referral if symptoms persist beyond 6-12 weeks or if neurological symptoms develop.

evidence.snippet: rvices without needing a referral from a GP. Get NHS help for back or joint problems Treatments for tennis elbow Tennis elbow usually gets better after resting your arm for a few weeks, but it can sometimes last longer. A GP may give you anti-inflammatory tablets. They may also refer you for physiotherapy if your symptoms have not improved after trying treat…

evidence.title: Elbow Injuries | Elbow Disorders | MedlinePlus
evidence.snippet: bone, cartilage, ligaments and fluid. Muscles and tendons help the elbow joint move. When any of these structures is hurt or diseased, you have elbow problems. Many things can make your elbow hurt. A common cause is tendinitis , an inflammation or injury to the tendons that attach muscle to bone. Tendinitis of the elbow is a sports injury , often from playin…

evidence.title: Juvenile Arthritis | Still's Disease | JRA | MedlinePlus
evidence.snippet: oms can vary depending on the type of JA. The symptoms of most types include joint pain, swelling, warmth, and stiffness. Your child may not complain of pain or stiffness, but one early sign of JA may be limping or clumsiness in the morning or after resting. Larger joints, such as the knee, may be swollen. Certain types of JA can cause a high fever , swollen…
